sections.heading: PART II Statement of Policy
sections.title: Policy statement for cybersecurity in banking sector
sections.summary: This section establishes the purpose and scope of cybersecurity policies for banking institutions in Kenya. It mandates that institutions develop strategies to mitigate cyber risks, promote stability in the banking sector, combat cybercrime, protect critical information infrastructure, and maintain public trust. The guidance applies to all banking institutions licensed under the Banking Act (Cap. 488) but does not replace existing regulatory obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets the foundational policy framework for cybersecurity that institutions must follow when designing data transfer mechanisms. It emphasizes governance structures and risk management that impact how data is handled across borders and with third parties.

requirements.text: This Guidance Note outlines the minimum requirements that institutions shall build upon in the development and implementation of strategies, policies, procedures and related activities aimed at mitigating cyber risk.
Create a safer and more secure cyberspace that underpins information system security priorities and promote stability of the Kenyan banking sector
Establish a coordinated approach to the prevention and combating of cybercrime
Up-scaling of identification and protection of critical information infrastructure
Promotion of compliance with appropriate technical and operational cybersecurity standards
Development of requisite skills, continuous building of capacity and promote a culture of fostering a strong interplay between policy, leveraging on technology to do business and risk management
Maintenance of public trust and confidence in the financial system

sections.source_quote: This Guidance Note outlines the minimum requirements that institutions shall build upon in the development and implementation of strategies, policies, procedures and related activities aimed at mitigating cyber risk.

sections.heading: PART III Specific Requirements
sections.title: Specific cybersecurity requirements for banking institutions
sections.summary: This section details specific cybersecurity requirements for banking institutions, including governance responsibilities, regular independent assessments, outsourcing protocols, and training programs. It mandates that the board and senior management establish cybersecurity strategies, implement risk management frameworks, conduct regular penetration tests, and ensure third-party service providers comply with cybersecurity standards. The guidance emphasizes continuous monitoring and incident response mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies technical and operational requirements for cybersecurity that directly impact data transfer and storage practices. It mandates robust governance, regular testing, and third-party oversight, which are critical for assessing cross-border data transfers and ensuring compliance with international standards.

requirements.text: Board of Directors should understand the nature of their institution's business and cyber threats
Board of Directors should oversee cultivation of ethical governance culture and awareness
Board of Directors should engage management in establishing cybersecurity vision and strategic direction
Board of Directors should allocate adequate cybersecurity budget based on institution structure
Board of Directors should review management's determination of cybersecurity preparedness alignment with cyber risks
Board of Directors should establish cybersecurity risk ownership and management accountability
Board of Directors should approve and continuously review cybersecurity strategy, governance charter, policy and framework
Board of Directors should ensure cybersecurity policy applies to all operating entities, including subsidiaries and geographic regions
Board of Directors should review implementation of cybersecurity framework and implementation plan at least once in 12 months
Board of Directors should incorporate cybersecurity as a standard agenda in Board meetings
Senior Management should implement board-approved cybersecurity strategy, policy and framework
Senior Management should understand cyber organizational scope and identify cyber threats
Senior Management should ensure creation of mitigation and recovery procedures for cyber risk incidents
Senior Management should continuously improve collection, analysis and reporting of cybercrime information
Senior Management should oversee deployment of strong authentication measures
Senior Management should ensure provision of skilled staff for cybersecurity with enhanced background checks
Senior Management should ensure timely and regular reporting to the board on cyber risk status
Senior Management should establish a cybersecurity benchmarking framework with Board's endorsement
Senior Management should incorporate cybersecurity as a standard agenda in Senior Management meetings
Institutions should provide regular reports of cybersecurity posture to the board
Institutions should document cybersecurity incident response plan with roles, responsibilities, incident detection and assessment, reporting, and escalation strategies
Institutions should collaborate with other institutions and security agencies to share latest cyber threats
Institutions should create a post-incident analysis framework to determine corrective actions
Institutions should oversee evaluation and management of risks introduced by third party service providers
CISO should oversee and implement institution's cybersecurity program and enforce cybersecurity policy
CISO should ensure institution maintains current enterprise-wide knowledge base of users, devices, applications and their relationships
CISO should ensure information systems meet institution's needs and ICT strategy complies with business strategies
CISO should design cybersecurity controls with consideration of users at all levels
CISO should organize professional cyber-related trainings to improve staff technical proficiency
CISO should ensure regular and comprehensive cyber risk assessments are conducted
CISO should ensure adequate processes for monitoring IT systems to detect cybersecurity events
CISO should report to CEO on an agreed interval but not less than once per quarter on confidentiality, integrity, availability of information systems
CISO should report on detailed exceptions to approved cybersecurity policies and procedures
CISO should report on assessment of effectiveness of approved cybersecurity program
CISO should report on all material cybersecurity events affecting the institution
Institutions should carry out independent cyber threat test at least once a year
Institutions should incorporate qualified ICT Auditors within Internal Audit team
Internal ICT Auditors should continuously review and report on cyber risks and controls of ICT systems
Internal ICT Auditors should assess design and effectiveness of cybersecurity framework
Internal ICT Auditors should conduct regular independent threat and vulnerability assessment tests
Internal ICT Auditors should report to the board findings of assessments
Internal ICT Auditors should conduct comprehensive penetration tests
Risk Management Function should assess cybersecurity risks and determine alignment with institution's risk appetite
Risk Management Function should monitor current and emerging cybersecurity risks
Risk Management Function should collaborate with system administrators to ensure appropriate control design
Risk Management Function should maintain comprehensive cyber risk registers
Risk Management Function should ensure implementation of cyber and information risk management strategy
Risk Management Function should safeguard confidentiality, integrity and availability of information
Risk Management Function should establish comprehensive inventory of IT assets by business criticality
Risk Management Function should quantify potential impact by assessing residual cyber risk
Risk Management Function should report all enterprise risks consistently and comprehensively to the board
Risk Management Function should conduct red team exercises
External Auditors should ensure IT audit scope includes understanding institution's IT infrastructure
External Auditors should understand extent of institution's automated controls as they relate to financial reporting
External Auditors should conduct independent threat and vulnerability assessment
External Auditors should conduct comprehensive review of approved cybersecurity strategy and policy
External Auditors should conduct comprehensive penetration tests

sections.heading: PART III Specific Requirements (cont.)
sections.title: Outsourcing and Third-Party Cybersecurity Requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement robust governance for outsourcing agreements, including due diligence on service providers, documented contracts, and monitoring of service delivery. They must treat outsourcing agreements as critical infrastructure, select vendors based on compliance and risk assessments, ensure computing resources are secured, and require service providers to comply with applicable legal frameworks. Institutions must also understand third-party risks, perform analytics on their outsourcing portfolio, collaborate with third parties to mitigate risks, and monitor for changes in third parties' cyber posture.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific cybersecurity controls for third-party relationships, requiring institutions to assess and manage risks associated with outsourcing to ensure data security and compliance during cross-border data transfers involving third parties.

requirements.text: Have in place adequate governance of outsourcing agreements including due diligence on prospective service providers, documented outsourcing agreements and adequate monitoring of service delivery.
Consider all outsourcing agreements as critical infrastructure for regulation and protection for purposes of security of the banking sector and the economy at large.
Select their vendors based on compliance and risk assessments.
Ensure all computing resources are secured including registrations, licensing, compliance and verification.
Ensure all outsourcing contracts require service providers to comply with applicable legal and regulatory frameworks.
Understand the inherent risk arising from each third party.
Perform analytics on an institution's outsourcing portfolio to understand which pose the most relative risk to an institution.
Work collaboratively with third parties to mitigate risks that pose the most risk to an institution.
Monitor contracted third parties for changes in their business and cyber posture including expansions, divestitures, breaches and new attacks that may alter the third parties' exposure.
Service Level Agreements should have robust provisions in relation to security, service availability, performance metrics or penalties.
Develop exit management strategies and contingency plans.

sections.source_quote: Have in place adequate governance of outsourcing agreements including due diligence on prospective service providers, documented outsourcing agreements and adequate monitoring of service delivery.
